source_metadata.title_raw: Andrew J. Pinasco - A-18-021 - March 5, 2018 - Stockton

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 5, 2018

Andrew J. Pinasco
Neumiller & Beardslee
Post Office Box 20
Stockton, CA 95201-3010

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-021

Dear Mr. Pinasco:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Trustee Brett Tholborn, a member of the Board of Trustees for Reclamation District 1608 (the "District"), regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Under the Act, may Trustee Tholborn take part in the District's decisions to fund the dredging of 14-Mile Slough?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Trustee Tholborn may take part in decisions concerning funding the dredging project of 14-Mile Slough.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

For purposes of this letter, the facts included in our previous letter to your office, Stovall Advice Letter, No. 08-112, are incorporated by reference, excepting the facts related to Trustee Tholborn's unusable boat dock.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-021
Page No. 2

In 2008, Trustee Tholborn requested advice regarding the dredging of 14-Mile Slough.
(Stovall Advice Letter, supra.) In an area along the portion of the 14-Mile Slough included in the
dredging project, 46 landowners have boat docks attached to their residential lots, which provide
the landowners with access to 14-Mile Slough and the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta. Trustee
Tholborn is one of the 46 landowners with a boat dock along the 14-Mile Slough and, at that time,
his boat dock was inaccessible and unusable due to silt and mud accumulation along the slough.

In 2008, we advised your office that Trustee Tholborn had a disqualifying conflict of
interest due to the estimated $30,000 to $50,000 increase in the resale value of his property from the
restored accessibility of his boat dock resulting from the dredging project. (Stovall Advice Letter,
supra.) As a result, Trustee Tholborn has refrained from participating in any decision related to the
dredging project by recusing himself from Board of Trustees meetings when the Board considered
the topic of the dredging project.

Since 2008, Trustee Tholborn has invested approximately $40,000 in upgrades to his boat
dock. Trustee Tholborn's upgrades provide him full access and complete use of his boat dock,
including marine access to the water. Specifically, Trustee Tholborn has 1.5 feet of draft at low tide,
and currently accesses the slough by watercraft, which he moors at his dock. Trustee Tholborn's use
of his dock is not inhibited by the current depth of the slough, which has yet to be dredged.

You indicated that the neighborhood containing Trustee Tholborn's property is fully
occupied by residences. You noted that the District's decision to fund the project would not
increase traffic, effect sightline view, or alter the neighborhood's intensity of use. You also stated that noise
levels in the neighborhood would not significantly increase as the boat docks are located at the rear
of the residential lots, on the waterside of the levee, which acts as a sound wall.

At the time of your current request for advice, the dredging of 14-Mile Slough has
incrementally progressed, but the project has yet to be completed and there are more decisions to be
made before the project's completion.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103, Regulation
18700(a).) Specific to your facts, Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest
may arise, including:

• An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

File No. A-18-021
Page No. 3

• The official's interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.²

Trustee Tholborn has an investment interest of $2,000 or more in his residence along 14-Mile Slough. Accordingly, Trustee Tholborn has an interest in real property.

Foreseeability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if it is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).)³ For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Based on the provided facts, Trustee Tholborn's property is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue.

For properties not explicitly involved in decisions, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under which a reasonably foreseeable effect on the interests is material. Most pertinent to the provided facts, a foreseeable effect will be material if a decision:

"(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision;

[‖]...[‖]

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;

___________________________

² An effect on a public official's interest in his or her own personal finances, or those of the public official's immediate family, is not considered separately if the decision also affects the public official's interest in real property. (Regulation 18702.5(c).) As the decision at issue also affects Trustee Tholborn's interest in real property, we do not analyze his interest in his personal finances.

³ A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

File No. A-18-021
Page No. 4

"(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are
analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under
these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the
official's property; or

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was
of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market
value of the official's property."

As concluded in our previous letter, the slough is a storm drainage or similar facility.
(Stovall Advice Letter, supra.) However, to the extent that the dredging project involves
construction of, or improvements to, 14-Mile Slough, Trustee Tholborn's alterations to his boat
dock have eliminated the potential benefit he would have received in 2008 from the dredging of the
slough. Specifically, the dredging project will no longer result in a potential increase in the resale
value of Trustee Tholborn's home and lot by $30,000 to $50,000. As Trustee Tholborn has removed
the potential benefit to his property, his property will not receive "new or improved services"
distinguishable from those received by the 46 similarly situated residential properties along 14-Mile
Slough with boat docks attached.

In addition, your facts suggest that the dredging project would not substantially alter the
view, noise levels, traffic levels, or intensity of use of Trustee Tholborn's property or the
surrounding residential neighborhood. Further, Trustee Tholborn has full use of his dock and access
to the slough by watercraft, which he moors at his dock, despite the fact that the slough has not yet
been dredged. As such, your facts do not indicate that the District's decision to fund the dredging
project would influence the market value of Trustee Tholborn's property.

Finally, as currently described, there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on Trustee Tholborn's property. Accordingly, a decision
to fund the dredging project does not appear to have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on
Trustee Tholborn's real property interest and he is not prohibited from participating in the Board of
Trustees' decisions to fund the project under the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,
Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Sara K. Puricelli
Counsel, Legal Division

SKP:jgl

STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 5, 2018

Bill Peake
Pacific Grove City Councilmember
300 Forest Avenue
Pacific Grove, CA 93950

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-023

Dear Mr. Peake:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes our facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you, a Pacific Grove City Councilmember, from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the proposed relocation of a utility cabinet within approximately 475 feet of your residence?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your residence.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Pacific Grove will soon consider whether to enter into a contract with Monterey Water, a regional agency that provides wastewater treatment and recycling services to the City and other local government agencies, to relocate a utility cabinet used to operate a sewage lift station on the seaward side of Oceanside Boulevard. The utility cabinet and lift station components of the City's sewer collection system, and are owned by Monterey One Water. The

_____________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-023
Page No. 2

utility cabinet would be relocated to City-owned property at the northern end of Esplanade Park,
across Oceanview Boulevard from the lift station.

Your residence, a single-family home within a neighborhood of single-family homes, is
located within 475 feet of the proposed location for the relocation of the utility cabinet. You own
your residence, and it is valued at more than $2,000. Although the precise design of the utility
cabinet has not been finalized, you state that the height, length, or width of the utility cabinet will
not exceed several feet. Due to intervening homes, trees, and topography, the relocated utility
cabinet would not be visible from your residence.

ANALYSIS

Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Act prohibit a public official from taking part in a
decision in which the official would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an
official's financial interest. Section 87103 identifies the interests that may give rise to a
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act. You have a real-property interest in your residence
under Section 87103(b) because you have a direct interest in it worth $2,000 or more.

Foreseeability & Materiality

For a financial interest that is not a named party in, or the subject of, the decision at issue,
the financial effect of that decision on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation
18701(b).) Your residence is not a named party in, or the subject of, the decisions relating to the
proposed relocation of the utility cabinet.

Under the Act's 500-foot rule, set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)(11), a decision's effect on
an official's real-property interest is material unless there are sufficient facts to indicate that there
will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property. The relocated utility
cabinet will not be visible from your residence due to intervening homes, trees, and topography.
The facts presented provide no indication that the decisions at issue would have a measurable
impact on your residence. Thus, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division
